title: Preprocess, compile, assemble and link

publication.changeSummary: Language-specific C: Beginner to Intermediate draft for Preprocess, compile, assemble and link; human standards, technical, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: GNU C Language Introduction and Reference Manual; accessed 2026-07; provisions applicable to Preprocess, compile, assemble and link
sources.claim: C source passes through preprocessing, translation to object code, and linking with required definitions before the operating environment can load an executable.

canonicalExplanation.summary: C source passes through preprocessing, translation to object code, and linking with required definitions before the operating environment can load an executable.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A macro and include directive are handled before the compiler checks expressions and emits an object file, then the linker resolves printf from the C library. Your goal is to diagnose whether a supplied failure occurs during preprocessing, compilation or linking. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: pf computing execution; pf tools terminal editor; Translation unit and main. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language rule and operational model
sections.body: C source passes through preprocessing, translation to object code, and linking with required definitions before the operating environment can load an executable. This rule is applied to concrete C source and observable behavior, not treated as a vocabulary definition alone.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: Toolchains can combine phases, perform link-time optimization, or emit intermediate forms, but preprocessing and translation rules still determine observable program meaning. Therefore the draft explicitly rejects this beginner failure model: If every .c file compiles without error, the complete program must already be executable.

includes: Relate source, object files, libraries and executable output.
Diagnose whether a supplied failure occurs during preprocessing, compilation or linking.
excludes: Toolchains can combine phases, perform link-time optimization, or emit intermediate forms, but preprocessing and translation rules still determine observable program meaning.

outcomes: Diagnose whether a supplied failure occurs during preprocessing, compilation or linking.

examples.title: Preprocess, compile, assemble and link: defined case
examples.situation: A macro and include directive are handled before the compiler checks expressions and emits an object file, then the linker resolves printf from the C library.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the concrete source or state: A macro and include directive are handled before the compiler checks expressions and emits an object file, then the linker resolves printf from the C library.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the conclusion: Successful compilation produces translation-unit objects; the linker must still resolve every required external definition before an executable exists.
examples.result: The defined result follows because Successful compilation produces translation-unit objects; the linker must still resolve every required external definition before an executable exists.

examples.title: Preprocess, compile, assemble and link: boundary case
examples.situation: Each source file compiles successfully, but the final link fails because one declared function has no definition in any linked object.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed source or state: Each source file compiles successfully, but the final link fails because one declared function has no definition in any linked object.

Step 2 — Keep this standard boundary visible: Toolchains can combine phases, perform link-time optimization, or emit intermediate forms, but preprocessing and translation rules still determine observable program meaning.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting rule: If every .c file compiles without error, the complete program must already be executable.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Diagnose whether a supplied failure occurs during preprocessing, compilation or linking.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: If every .c file compiles without error, the complete program must already be executable.
misconceptions.correction: Successful compilation produces translation-unit objects; the linker must still resolve every required external definition before an executable exists.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using concrete C rules, explain why this belief fails: If every .c file compiles without error, the complete program must already be executable.

analogy.example: A manually managed workshop can model Preprocess, compile, assemble and link: every tool, address, buffer, and acquired resource has an explicit valid range and owner.
usefulFor: Making low-level state, bounds, representation, ownership, and cleanup responsibilities concrete
limitations: C abstract-machine rules, undefined behavior, pointer provenance, translation, and implementation variation are stricter than a physical workshop analogy.
Toolchains can combine phases, perform link-time optimization, or emit intermediate forms, but preprocessing and translation rules still determine observable program meaning.

Line 3: give the result and reject the common mistake.

recap.summary: You should now be able to diagnose whether a supplied failure occurs during preprocessing, compilation or linking. Keep this boundary in view: Toolchains can combine phases, perform link-time optimization, or emit intermediate forms, but preprocessing and translation rules still determine observable program meaning.
retrievalPrompts: Explain Preprocess, compile, assemble and link using a concrete C rule rather than the optional analogy.
Why is this claim incorrect: If every .c file compiles without error, the complete program must already be executable.
Apply the checkpoint 'linker' to the boundary case from memory.
